items.mistake: Indexing a linked list as if it were an array (`node[i]`).
items.fix: Walk explicitly; if you need O(1) access by index, use an array (Python list).
items.why: Each step requires following one pointer — index i costs O(i).

items.mistake: Losing a reference to the predecessor when deleting a node.
items.fix: Track `prev` while walking, or use a doubly-linked list with a node reference.
items.why: Removing `cur` from a singly-linked list requires updating `prev.next`; without `prev` you'd have to re-walk.
items.example:
```python
def delete(head, target):
    prev, cur = None, head
    while cur and cur.val != target:
        prev, cur = cur, cur.next
    if cur is None: return head
    if prev is None: return cur.next
    prev.next = cur.next
    return head
```

items.mistake: Creating a cycle by accident (node points back into earlier chain).
items.fix: Use Floyd's tortoise-and-hare cycle detection or set the obsolete pointer to `None` carefully.
items.why: A cycle turns iteration into an infinite loop and is hard to spot in logs.
items.example:
```python
def has_cycle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow is fast: return True
    return False
```

items.mistake: Implementing your own LRU when one already exists.
items.why: These leverage CPython's C-level doubly-linked list machinery — faster and battle-tested.
